entry.title: Microsoft 365 MFA Bypass, Jamf Salesforce Exposure, and Circles Surveillance Exports
entry.content:
This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by how users, vendors, governments, and analysts operationalize them. Mitiga detailed an attempted business email compromise campaign against a Microsoft 365 organization in which an executive account was accessed from multiple geographic locations, including Singapore, Dubai, and San Jose, California. The initial compromise used an adversary-in-the-middle phishing technique that gave the attacker access to the executive’s account and mailbox. The notable persistence mechanism was the registration of a second Microsoft Authenticator app for the compromised user. Mitiga reported that Microsoft 365 did not require an MFA re-challenge when authentication methods were changed in the Security Info section if the existing session token had already satisfied MFA, allowing the attacker to weaken the account’s effective protection after initial access. Jamf separately disclosed that a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed an unauthorized party to access data in Jamf’s Salesforce instance through Klue’s integration. Jamf said its products and customer service operations were not affected, that it disabled the integration after notification, and that it had no evidence of lateral movement. The company warned that contact information stored in Salesforce could be used in phishing campaigns impersonating Jamf employees or IT professionals. Cisco Talos used its newsletter to emphasize that possession of accurate security information does not reliably translate into secure decisions. The article framed security behavior as a human and organizational problem shaped by budgets, workloads, priorities, and operational constraints, even when established controls such as segmentation, backups, MFA, and control validation are well understood. The same Talos roundup referenced several credential- and access-focused security headlines, including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe compromise, a credential-harvesting operation affecting more than 30,000 Fortinet devices, and Phantom Stealer activity targeting browser credentials. Together with the Mitiga and Jamf items, these reports show continued attacker focus on identity systems, browser-stored secrets, SaaS data stores, and post-compromise opportunities for phishing or persistence. The Record reported on Human Rights Watch findings that Bulgaria approved export licenses from 2018 through 2023 allowing surveillance firm Circles to sell products to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in countries described as repressive. Approved destinations reportedly included El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco, and Panama. The tools identified in the report included Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls, and messages; Landmark, which can geolocate mobile phones in real time; and Voice Over Location Enabler software, which can remotely intercept incoming and outgoing voice calls using the SS7 protocol. Human Rights Watch said the records cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light, while Bulgaria stated that exports contradicting national, European, and international commitments are not allowed. The European Commission said member states remain responsible for dual-use export licensing decisions. Talos also described a reverse engineering workflow pairing local AI agents with tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live COM interface. The approach allows analysts to issue natural-language prompts for tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the local workstation. The discussion reflects a broader shift in malware analysis and security operations tooling, where AI-assisted workflows are being explored for analyst productivity without necessarily sending proprietary or sensitive samples to external services. In this case, the emphasis was on local orchestration of existing reverse engineering capabilities rather than cloud-based analysis.

Advisory: Persistent MFA Circumvention in an Advanced BEC Campaign on Microsoft 365 Targets — Should Read

Mitiga found that attackers using AiTM phishing can compromise Microsoft 365 accounts protected by MFA and then add their own Microsoft Authenticator app without a fresh MFA challenge, giving them persistent access. The key takeaway is that organizations should not rely on MFA alone and should enforce device-based Conditional Access while monitoring for new authentication method registrations.

  • Mitiga investigated an attempted business email compromise attack against a Microsoft 365 organization in which an executive’s account was accessed from locations including Singapore, Dubai, and San Jose, California.
  • The initial compromise used an adversary-in-the-middle phishing technique that gave the attacker access to the executive’s account and mailbox.
  • Mitiga found that the attacker set up a second Microsoft Authenticator app for the compromised user, creating persistence and weakening the protection provided by MFA.
  • The article states that Microsoft 365 does not require an MFA re-challenge when a user changes authentication methods in the Security Info section if the session token was previously satisfied.
  • Mitiga recommends adding another layer of defense through device-based Conditional Access and monitoring for new authentication method registrations.

Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says — Should Read

Human Rights Watch says Bulgaria approved exports by surveillance firm Circles to multiple governments with records of repression, enabling tools that can intercept communications and track phones. The report raises fresh concerns that EU export controls are failing to stop surveillance technology from reaching abusive regimes.

  • Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records from 2018 through 2023 showing that Bulgaria allowed the surveillance firm Circles to sell technology to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for repressing citizens.
  • The approved destinations included El Salvador, the United Arab Emirates, Serbia, Azerbaijan, Guatemala, Bahrain, Jordan, Malaysia, Morocco and Panama.
  • Circles sold tools including Pixcell, which can capture internet data, phone calls and messages, Landmark, which geolocates mobile phones in real time, and Voice Over Location Enabler software, which can remotely intercept incoming and outgoing voice calls using SS7 protocol.
  • Human Rights Watch said the records cast the European Union’s export control regime in a harsh light because it is meant to stop the sale of sophisticated surveillance technology to abusive governments.
  • The Bulgarian government told Human Rights Watch that exports contradicting national, European and international commitments, including human rights protections, are not allowed, while the European Commission said member states are solely responsible for dual-use export licensing decisions.

Klue Third-Party Cybersecurity Incident — Should Read

Jamf disclosed that a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed unauthorized access to Jamf Salesforce data via Klue’s integration, but Jamf says its products and customer service operations were not affected. Jamf disabled the integration, is investigating the exposed business data, and warned customers to watch for phishing attempts using Salesforce contact information.

  • Jamf said a cybersecurity incident at third-party vendor Klue allowed an unauthorized party to access Jamf’s Salesforce instance data through Klue’s integration.
  • Jamf stated that the incident did not affect Jamf’s products and did not impact its ability to serve customers.
  • After receiving notice from Klue, Jamf immediately disabled Klue’s Salesforce integration and said it has no evidence of lateral movement.
  • Jamf engaged cybersecurity experts, notified law enforcement, and is reviewing what data was impacted.
  • Jamf warned customers that the unauthorized party may use contact information stored in Salesforce to conduct phishing campaigns while posing as Jamf employees or IT professionals.

Close Encounters of the Human Kind — Optional

Cisco Talos argues that cybersecurity improvement depends less on simply giving people the right information and more on accounting for real-world constraints, behavior, and priorities. The practical takeaway is to focus on proven basics like MFA, patching, segmentation, backups, and validating controls rather than assuming awareness alone will drive action.

  • The article argues that having the right security information does not necessarily cause people or organizations to take the right actions because decisions are shaped by budgets, workloads, priorities, and other constraints.
  • The author highlights longstanding security controls such as segmentation, backups, MFA, and validation that controls are working as important protections for organizations.
  • Cisco Talos described a reverse engineering approach that pairs local AI agents with tools such as the VB6 disassembler vbdec through a live COM interface.
  • The vbdec approach allows analysts to use natural language prompts to automate tasks such as decompiling functions and building call graphs while keeping sensitive binaries on the local workstation.
  • The newsletter summarizes security headlines including ShinyHunters claiming a Council of Europe hack, a credential-harvesting operation affecting more than 30,000 Fortinet devices, and Phantom Stealer targeting browser credentials.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by identity-centric compromise risks, third-party SaaS exposure, and renewed scrutiny of commercial surveillance technology exports. The reporting also highlights a persistent theme for defenders: security outcomes are shaped not only by technical controls, but by...

entry.title: ChatGPT for Science Leak, AI Guardrail Gaps, and Executive Phishing Tradecraft
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary underscores gaps between provider-native AI controls and organizational SecOps visibility needs. Separately, an authorized assessment shows how public construction details and journalist impersonation can be used to drive executive engagement and downstream third-party exposure. BleepingComputer reported that OpenAI appears to be testing a new subscription and experience called ChatGPT for Science, based on references found in the ChatGPT web build. The article notes that availability remains unclear and that the offering may be restricted to verified institutes or universities rather than broadly available to all users. OpenAI already offers ChatGPT for personal, Teams, business, and enterprise customers, and recently announced GPT-Rosalind, a specialized model for enterprise-scale life sciences research delivered through a trusted-access deployment structure. The reporting reflects a broader trend toward domain-specific AI services for scientific and enterprise research workflows. In the life sciences context, specialized models and restricted deployment structures indicate that vendors are attempting to balance advanced research capability with controlled access models. The article does not state that ChatGPT for Science has been publicly launched, only that references in the web build suggest testing is underway. Netskope separately argued that native AI guardrails from providers such as Amazon Bedrock, Anthropic, and OpenAI often align more closely with provider liability and intellectual property protection than with enterprise SecOps requirements. The article states that these controls may block legitimate cybersecurity research while still allowing sensitive prompts or intellectual property to be sent to third-party providers. It also describes observability gaps where refusals or silent model behavior changes may not clearly identify which guardrail was triggered or why. NetSPI described an authorized social engineering assessment targeting a client’s executive leadership team. Testers used open-source research about a planned new facility to build a journalist impersonation pretext focused on alleged hazardous waste disposal at the construction site. The assessment used a ProtonMail account in a journalist’s name, a lookalike domain, and an Evilginx server designed to mimic the client’s Microsoft login flow and capture authentication data during the test. The scenario gained traction when an executive responsible for the construction project responded to the pretext. After the testers claimed there was a Teams issue, the executive received a phishing link and forwarded it to two contracting firms involved in the project. NetSPI stopped the phishing session and notified its point of contact because those contracting firms were outside the authorized engagement scope. The case illustrates how adversary-style pretexts can combine public business developments, executive responsibilities, and time-sensitive reputational concerns to increase credibility. It also shows how a phishing operation aimed at one organization can quickly propagate into adjacent vendors or contractors when trusted business relationships are involved.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by two related themes: enterprise exposure around AI adoption and realistic social engineering tradecraft against senior leadership. Reporting highlights continued specialization of commercial AI offerings for research environments, while security commentary...

entry.title: Malicious VS Code Extensions, Crypto Clippers, CISA BOD 26-04, and Google IP Ad Targeting
entry.content: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor extensions reaching privileged developer environments and a crypto clipper campaign using coordinated fake legitimacy signals across software-hosting and media platforms. Socket announced beta support in Socket Firewall for blocking malicious VS Code Marketplace and Open VSX extensions before they reach developer workstations. The capability routes editor marketplace requests through Socket’s proxy and filters search, install, update, and VSIX download flows based on threat verdicts, extending supply chain controls into IDE extension ecosystems. The announcement cites GitHub’s May 2026 disclosure that attackers compromised an employee device through the malicious Nx Console 18.95.0 VS Code extension and exfiltrated roughly 3,800 GitHub-internal repositories. Socket emphasized that editor extensions may access source code, terminals, files, credentials, tokens, and internal systems, making compromised extensions a high-impact intrusion path inside development environments. Check Point Research reported a crypto clipper campaign distributing a Rust-based clipboard hijacker disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots and crash-game predictors. The malware targets Windows and macOS users, monitors clipboard contents for cryptocurrency wallet address patterns, and replaces matching entries with attacker-controlled addresses from a hard-coded list. The campaign’s distribution model relies heavily on manufactured credibility. The actor used paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites, coordinated positive comments on VirusTotal, GitHub, SourceForge, and YouTube, and inflated indicators such as stars, forks, downloads, upvotes, and AI-generated narrator content. Check Point also reported use of EIN Presswire to market the tools’ claimed capabilities, resulting in syndication across partner news sites, primarily in the USA TODAY Network. Tenable published analysis on operationalizing CISA Binding Operational Directive 26-04, which supersedes BOD 19-02 and BOD 22-01. The directive shifts federal vulnerability management away from static severity scoring and toward remediation timelines based on four contextual risk variables: asset exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities status, exploit automation, and technical impact. According to Tenable, asset exposure is the highest-leverage variable in the directive’s model. Its analysis found that removing public exposure shifted 76.7% of CVEs from compressed remediation timelines into the deferral tier. The article also notes that BOD 26-04 introduces a forensic triage requirement for KEV-listed vulnerabilities that yield total system control, and Tenable states that 83% of actively exploited CVEs meet that impact threshold. Google has begun notifying advertisers that it will start using IP addresses for ad measurement and personalization across the European Economic Area, the UK, and Switzerland on or shortly after August 3, 2026. The company said IP addresses it already receives for traffic routing and ad delivery will also be used to identify devices for measurement and personalization. The article notes that IP addresses are considered personal data under GDPR in the EEA, the UK, and Switzerland, and that using automatically transmitted information such as IP addresses to identify devices is a building block of fingerprinting. Google plans to register under IAB Europe’s Transparency and Consent Framework for Feature 3, while its customer email states that advertisers remain bound by Google’s EU User Consent Policy and must obtain valid consent from users in affected regions.
entry.summary: This coverage window centers on trust boundaries that attackers and platform operators are increasingly exploiting or redefining: developer tooling, reputation systems, vulnerability prioritization, and user-identifying network metadata. The most security-relevant items involve malicious editor...

entry.title: ClickFix SmartRAT and Meow Campaigns, FortiBleed Leak, and UK CNI State Threats
entry.content: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting, and renewed warnings about state prepositioning in critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting site impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver SmartRAT. The lure presented a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA followed by a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt, pressuring victims into executing a PowerShell command that downloaded the malware. SmartRAT provides encrypted C2, remote screen and input control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR code interception, and persistence via scheduled tasks and a Windows service. SmartRAT is financially focused, monitoring window titles associated with Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency activity and alerting operators when victims interact with targeted services. ThreatLabz also found a weakness in the SmartRAT web C2 panel in which client-side authentication could be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values. Netskope Threat Labs separately described a macOS ClickFix campaign observed on May 31, 2026 that delivered an upgraded AppleScript payload labeled “Meow (DEBUG).” The infection chain remained fileless until persistence, using a victim-executed curl command to fetch a compressed stager and pipe AppleScript into osascript memory. The payload checks for Russian keyboard input, steals credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, files, gaming data, and Apple Notes, then persists as com.apple.accountsd for recurring C2 beacons and arbitrary command execution. A newly identified leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed apparent Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs worldwide. Researcher Bob Diachenko said the exposed server contained usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with references to major organizations including Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid. Kevin Beaumont reported confirming the authenticity of some administrative credentials and said the dataset appeared to originate from exported Fortinet configurations. The FortiBleed reporting attributed the activity to a Russian-speaking, multi-operator threat group that allegedly made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems. In parallel, Zscaler’s 2026 phishing reporting found that overall phishing volume in its cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and difficult to distinguish from legitimate activity. Zscaler identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and assessed 9% as malicious. Government phishing attempts increased 50% year over year to 138.5 million hits in 2025, while healthcare saw lower phishing counts but 95.2% of phishing activity over encrypted channels. Separate account takeover reporting emphasized attacker adaptation to MFA through fatigue tactics, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, browser-in-the-middle kits, session hijacking, and credential theft from unmanaged or compromised endpoints. Richard Horne, chief executive of the UK National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with roughly 75% believed to involve state actors. He warned that adversaries are prepositioning inside technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during conflict. Horne cited Volt Typhoon as the clearest example of this prepositioning model. The remarks coincided with UK government work on the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and an expected National Cyber Action Plan. Horne framed cybersecurity as a contest against capable opponents rather than only a risk-management discipline, emphasizing capability relative to adversaries. Ukraine has also been granted access to the European Union’s Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to request EU-approved expertise during major cyberattacks. The reserve, managed by ENISA, provides incident response, digital forensics, technical expertise, and recovery support when incidents exceed national capacity. Ukrainian officials said the country already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution work. Censys analyzed AdaptixC2, an open-source C2 framework whose default configuration exposes identifiable HTTP headers such as Server: AdaptixC2 and Adaptix-Version: v1.2 on unauthenticated error responses. As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts using default or near-default AdaptixC2 settings. The research identified beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting, as well as one host serving AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory. The exposed directory included a Linux installer that deployed timesync.bin and established 12 persistence mechanisms. Censys noted that even operators who changed the default Server header could remain detectable if they left the default 404 response body string intact. Cato Networks reported on a French-speaking attacker known as “Poisson,” who compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Before his Havoc C2 server went offline, the attacker installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to preserve access outside the original C2 channel. Cato captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket, with the intrusion involving VBScript, PowerShell, .NET loaders, Havoc Demon, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger. Silent Push described the preparation phase as the period in which threat actors build and stage domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign becomes active. The company argued that SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds generally rely on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, leaving this phase underdetected. The article stated that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational. Silent Push said its approach maps DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to identify Indicators of Future Attack, claiming an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of traditional SIEM alerts and more than 300 days in some APT cases.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting,...

entry.title: Mastra npm Takeover, CVE-2026-48907 Exploitation, and Crypto Hijacker Campaigns
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and productivity platforms, alongside ongoing concerns about exposure prioritization and public-sector cyber capacity. Mend reported a compromise of more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope, where an attacker republished packages with a new dependency on easy-day-js. Version 1.11.22 of easy-day-js introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper that retrieved a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000 and launched it as a detached hidden process communicating with 23.254.164.123:443. The second stage was described as harvesting environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, with affected installations treated in the reporting as fully compromised. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin. The JCE security team said the vulnerability allows unauthenticated PHP upload and execution, is actively exploited, has public exploit code available, and is being targeted through automated attacks. The flaw was fixed in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6, while the vendor noted that updating does not remediate sites already compromised before patching. Broader exposure data from Intruder underscored why these classes of issues remain operationally significant. Across 3,000 attack surfaces, 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed, 42% had an internet-reachable database, and 30% exposed files or information that should not have been public. MySQL, Postgres, API documentation, WordPress admin panels, and RDP were among the most common findings. Check Point Research analyzed a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker campaign disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor tools, and crash-game predictors. The actor used a WordPress phishing site as a landing hub and promoted payloads through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites. The campaign also inflated trust signals using coordinated or fake GitHub engagement, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube activity, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments. The Windows payload persisted by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, while the macOS variant used an unlocker.command script to remove quarantine attributes and installed a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive. Both monitored clipboard or pasteboard contents and replaced cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses. IRONSCALES reported a French-language invoice notification sent through Mailgun from an Ecocert accounting subdomain that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. The email linked to an Upflow billing portal URL containing a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration, allowing anyone with the tokenized link to access the portal without credentials. The invoice requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC details, while a tracking pixel confirmed mailbox activity. BleepingComputer reported that India restricted Telegram access nationwide until June 22 after the National Testing Agency said the platform was used to sell access to leaked exam materials. The disruption extended beyond India, including to users in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked globally via FLAG Telecom. Researchers confirmed the route hijack but said they found no evidence that the wider disruption was deliberate, while civil society groups criticized the block as disproportionate. Microsoft confirmed it is investigating Office launch issues after Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026. The issue affects third-party applications using OLE automation to open Office apps or documents, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access, and may fail without an error message. User reports named affected applications such as CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, and Softdent. Operationally, The Record covered Sen. Mark Warner’s letter warning of CISA staffing cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators. Separately, Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program for managed SSE and SASE delivery, while a Hacker News article framed adversarial exposure validation as a way to distinguish theoretical exposure from reachable and consequential risk within Continuous Threat Exposure Management.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and...
